title: My Machine Is Making Unusual Noises: Pump, Valve, and Scale Diagnostics
hero_image_alt: Side profile of an Arco Studio Pro espresso machine on a wooden countertop, the internal pump area subtly illuminated through a translucent side panel, a hand reaching toward the power switch, dramatic directional lighting from the left creating depth, a calm kitchen environment in soft focus behind
intro: Espresso machines are not silent appliances — pumps hum, solenoids click, and boilers occasionally gurgle as they heat. But when your machine starts making sounds it has never made before — loud buzzing, grinding, chattering, hissing, or knocking — it is telling you something needs attention. Most unusual noises have straightforward causes: air in the pump, scale buildup in the boiler, a stuck valve, or a loose fitting. Identifying the type, timing, and location of the noise is the key to diagnosing the issue before it becomes a costly repair.

body.heading: Pump Noises: Buzzing, Chattering, and Screaming
body.content: The pump is the heart of your espresso machine, and it is also the most common source of unusual noise. The type of pump determines what sounds are normal and what signals a problem. The Arco Primo, Nano, and Viaggio use vibration pumps — electromagnetic solenoid pumps that oscillate a piston at mains frequency to create pressure. A normal vibration pump produces a steady, medium-pitched hum or buzz during brewing. If that hum becomes dramatically louder, higher pitched, or takes on a chattering, rattling quality, the most likely cause is air in the system. Air enters the pump when the water reservoir runs dry, when a water line fitting is loose, or after the machine has been unused for an extended period and water has evaporated or drained from the lines. To bleed air from a vibration pump, fill the reservoir, turn on the machine, and run water through the group head and steam wand alternately for 15 to 20 seconds each until the flow is steady and bubble-free and the pump sound returns to its normal hum. If the pump is screaming — an unusually high-pitched whine — it may be running dry even though the reservoir appears full. Check that the intake tube inside the reservoir is submerged and not clogged with debris. The Arco Doppio, Studio, Studio Pro, and Ufficio use rotary pumps, which are quieter, smoother, and connect directly to a water line. A normal rotary pump produces a low, smooth whir. A grinding or growling sound from a rotary pump can indicate worn internal vanes or bearings — a less common issue that requires professional service. A sudden loud knock when the pump starts or stops is usually the solenoid valve actuating and is normal on machines with three-way valves.

body.heading: Boiler Noises: Gurgling, Rumbling, and Popping
body.content: Boiler noises are often related to scale — mineral deposits that accumulate on the boiler walls and heating element over time. As water heats, dissolved minerals precipitate out and form a hard, chalky layer on the hottest surfaces. When the heating element cycles on, it rapidly heats this scale layer, which can trap small pockets of water beneath it. As these pockets flash to steam, they produce popping, crackling, or knocking sounds — similar to a kettle that has not been descaled. This is the most common cause of new boiler noises on machines that have been in use for several months without descaling. The fix is straightforward: descale the machine following the procedure for your specific model. On the Arco Primo and Nano, use a citric acid solution cycled through the boiler and brew circuit. On the Arco Doppio and Studio, the built-in descaling program guides you through the process. After descaling, the noises should diminish or disappear as the scale layer is dissolved and flushed out. A gentle gurgling sound during heating is normal on all boiler-type machines — it is simply the sound of water circulating by convection as the boiler heats. However, loud, persistent rumbling or a sound like boiling water rolling violently can indicate that the thermostat or PID is allowing the boiler to overheat past its set point, causing the water inside to reach a rolling boil. This should not happen during normal brewing operation and may indicate a thermostat failure on the Primo or a PID sensor issue on the Doppio and Studio. If your machine sounds like a pot of water at a vigorous boil during normal operation, turn it off and have it inspected.

body.heading: Valve and Solenoid Noises: Clicking, Hissing, and Whistling
body.content: Espresso machines contain multiple valves — brew valves, solenoid valves, safety pressure relief valves, and anti-vacuum valves — each of which can produce distinct sounds. A single, firm click or thunk when you start or stop brewing is the three-way solenoid valve actuating. On the Arco Doppio, Studio, and Studio Pro, this valve opens to release residual pressure from the group head into the drip tray after brewing, which is why you see a small amount of water drain from the group after each shot. This click is entirely normal and, in fact, its absence would indicate a problem — a solenoid that fails to actuate will leave pressure in the group, making it difficult to remove the portafilter and leaving water pooled on the puck. A continuous hissing sound from the group head area when the machine is idle suggests that the brew valve or solenoid is not sealing completely, allowing pressurized water or steam to leak through. This is often caused by scale or coffee residue preventing the valve from closing fully, and a thorough descaling and back-flush may resolve it. If the hissing persists, the valve seat or seal may need replacement. A high-pitched whistling from the steam wand area, even when the steam knob is closed, usually indicates a worn steam valve seat or O-ring that is allowing a small amount of steam to escape. Tightening the steam knob may temporarily resolve it, but the valve packing or seat will eventually need rebuilding. On the Arco Ufficio, the auto-steam system has its own solenoid that can click rhythmically during automated milk steaming — this is normal operation as the machine modulates steam flow.

body.heading: Vibration, Rattling, and When to Call for Service
body.content: Some noises are not internal to the machine at all — they are the machine vibrating against its environment. Vibration pumps produce physical oscillation at 50 or 60 hertz depending on your mains frequency, and this vibration can transfer to the countertop, nearby objects, or loose panels on the machine itself. If your Arco Primo or Nano has developed a new rattling sound, check the basics first: is the drip tray seated properly? Is the water reservoir fully clicked into place? Is the machine sitting on a hard, flat surface? A silicone mat or rubber feet under the machine can dramatically reduce transmitted vibration. The cup warming tray on top of the Doppio and Studio can rattle if cups are left on it during brewing — remove them or place a cloth underneath. Loose internal screws or fittings can also develop over time as the machine's thermal cycling and vibration gradually work fasteners loose. If you hear a new, persistent rattle that you cannot attribute to external objects, it may be worth having a technician check internal fittings. Certain noises warrant immediate attention. A loud, continuous electrical buzzing that does not vary with pump operation could indicate a failing electrical component. Any noise accompanied by a burning smell should prompt you to turn off the machine immediately and unplug it. Water sounds inside the machine accompanied by dripping onto internal components suggest a failed hose connection or boiler fitting. And a sharp, sudden crack or pop followed by the machine losing power likely means a fuse or thermal cutoff has tripped — a safety mechanism that prevents further damage. For any of these scenarios, contact Arco customer support or an authorized service center rather than attempting self-repair.

key_takeaways: Loud or chattering vibration pump noise usually means air in the system — bleed it by running water through the group head and steam wand until the flow is steady.
Popping or crackling from the boiler area indicates scale buildup — descale the machine to dissolve mineral deposits on the heating element.
A single click when starting or stopping a shot is the normal sound of the three-way solenoid valve on the Doppio, Studio, and Studio Pro.
Persistent hissing when idle suggests a valve not sealing fully — try back-flushing first, then have the valve inspected if it continues.
Any noise accompanied by a burning smell, electrical buzzing unrelated to the pump, or visible water inside the machine warrants immediate shutdown and professional service.
